critic_raw:
BASELINE ASSESSMENT:

The 2.5 percentage-point MAPE improvement (8.4% to 5.9%) is the result of comparing two models across two years in which the demand-management environment changed materially. Three independent confounds are present.

Demand response: interruptible-load contracts with large industrial customers make peak-hour load more controllable and predictable. Forecasting a demand-response-modified grid is a different (and potentially easier) problem than forecasting an unconstrained grid.

Process nonstationarity: the time-of-use tariff revision structurally changed commercial cooling behavior. The summer load curve had a different shape in the second year. Year-over-year MAPE statistics are not directly comparable when the underlying process changed.

Weather input quality: switching to a weather vendor with better station coverage improves forecast accuracy through better inputs, not better modeling methodology. This input advantage is confounded with the model improvement in the production comparison.

The new ensemble model may genuinely be the better approach for the current grid environment. A matched historical evaluation — both models applied to the same intervals with the same weather data, evaluated separately on curtailment and non-curtailment days — would provide evidence for this claim.

Verdict: empirical_test_agreed.
